title: Data Exploration — Understanding Your Data Before Modelling
key_insight: Never train a model on data you haven't explored. EDA (Exploratory Data Analysis) finds data quality issues, reveals patterns, and guides feature engineering — all before writing a single model.
body:
## Loading and Inspecting Data

```python
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Load the Titanic dataset
df = sns.load_dataset('titanic')

# First things to check:
print(df.shape)         # (891, 15) — 891 rows, 15 columns
print(df.head())         # first 5 rows
print(df.dtypes)         # data types of each column
print(df.info())         # non-null counts + dtypes
print(df.describe())     # statistics for numeric columns
print(df.isnull().sum()) # missing values per column
print(df['survived'].value_counts())  # class distribution
```

## The 5 Questions to Answer in EDA

```
1. What are the data types?   (numeric, categorical, datetime, text)
2. How much is missing?       (nulls per column, patterns in missingness)
3. What are the distributions? (skew, outliers, bimodal?)
4. Are there obvious errors?  (negative ages, impossible values)
5. What correlates with the target? (which features matter)
```

## Visualising Distributions

```python
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))

# Distribution of numeric variable
plt.subplot(2, 2, 1)
df['age'].hist(bins=30)
plt.title('Age Distribution'); plt.xlabel('Age')

# Category counts
plt.subplot(2, 2, 2)
df['pclass'].value_counts().plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Passenger Class'); plt.xlabel('Class')

# Target vs feature
plt.subplot(2, 2, 3)
survival_by_class = df.groupby('pclass')['survived'].mean()
survival_by_class.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Survival Rate by Class'); plt.ylabel('Survival Rate')

# Boxplot: distribution per category
plt.subplot(2, 2, 4)
df.boxplot(column='age', by='survived')
plt.title('Age by Survival Status')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## Correlation Matrix

```python
# Numeric features only:
df_numeric = df.select_dtypes(include=['number'])

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(df_numeric.corr(), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt='.2f')
plt.title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.show()
```
common_mistakes: Skipping EDA and going straight to modelling — you'll build models on data with silent issues that make results untrustworthy
Looking at correlations before checking distributions — a correlation can be dominated by outliers
Not checking target distribution — class imbalance (99% class A, 1% class B) requires different metrics and preprocessing
mini_exercise: Load the Titanic dataset. In 5 lines of code, answer: (1) What percentage of passengers survived? (2) Which gender had higher survival rate? (3) What was the average age? (4) What percentage of 'age' values are missing? (5) Which passenger class had the highest survival rate?
real_world_usage: At Airbnb, data scientists spend 2-3 days doing EDA before writing any model code. They look for: outlier listings (£1 per night or £10,000 per night), geographic anomalies (listings in the ocean), and seasonal patterns. Problems found in EDA save weeks of modelling time.
explain_back_prompt: What are the 5 key questions EDA should answer? Why should you always check for missing values before modelling? What does a correlation matrix tell you?

build_task.title: Complete EDA on a real dataset
build_task.context: Structured EDA is a professional habit — this template works for any dataset.
steps: Load the Titanic or Iris dataset
Step 2: Missing values count and percentage per column
Step 3: Numeric distributions — histogram for each numeric column
Step 4: Categorical counts — bar chart for each categorical column
Step 6: Target analysis — survival rate by sex, class, and age group
Write 3 sentences summarising the most important findings
build_task.expected_output: 6+ visualisations and a written summary of key patterns.
